title: Your first decision
instruction:
Imagine a library. A child walks up to the counter and asks for a book from the adult section. The librarian checks their age. If they are old enough — they can borrow it. If not — they can't.

That is an `if` statement. It runs a block of code only when a condition is true.

```
if age >= 18 {
    fmt.Println("Adult section access granted")
}
```

Run this. Then change the age to 15 and run it again. Notice what changes.
successMessage: When the condition is true, the code inside runs. When it's false, Go skips it completely. That is all an if statement does.

title: Comparison operators
instruction:
The condition inside an `if` uses comparison operators to check relationships between values.

- `==` equal to
- `!=` not equal to
- `<`  less than
- `>`  greater than
- `<=` less than or equal to
- `>=` greater than or equal to

Run this. Then change `age` to different values and see which lines print.
successMessage: Six operators. Every condition you will ever write uses at least one of them.

title: if-else — two paths
hint: Try age := 10 first, then age := 25. See which branch runs each time.
successMessage: Two paths. One condition. Go picks the right one every time.

title: else if — more than two paths
instruction:
Sometimes two options are not enough. Libraries have different sections for different ages — picture books, middle grade, young adult, adult.

`else if` adds more branches. Go checks each condition from top to bottom and runs the first one that is true. The rest are skipped.

Change `age` to different values — try 4, 9, 14, 25 — and run each time.
hint: Change the age value and hit Run. Each age should land in a different branch.
successMessage: Go checks conditions in order and stops at the first true one. Order matters — always put the strictest condition first.

title: Logical operators — combine conditions
instruction:
Sometimes one condition is not enough. What if you need two things to be true at the same time?

- `&&` AND — both must be true
- `||` OR — at least one must be true
- `!`  NOT — flips true to false and false to true

```
if age >= 18 && hasLibraryCard {
    fmt.Println("Borrowing approved")
}
```

Run this. Then try changing `hasLibraryCard` to `false` and running again.
expectedOutput: Visit the front desk for assistance
successMessage: && needs both. || needs one. ! flips it. Three operators that make your conditions as precise as you need.

title: switch — membership tiers
instruction:
When you have one variable and many possible values, a long `else if` chain gets messy fast.

`switch` is the cleaner version. It checks the variable against each `case` and runs the matching one.

```
switch tier {
case "Gold":
    fmt.Println("Unlimited books, no late fees")
case "Silver":
    fmt.Println("10 books at a time")
}
```

Change `tier` to different values — Gold, Silver, Bronze — and run each time.
hint: Change the tier := value to see different cases run.
starter:
package main

import "fmt"

func main() {
	tier := "Gold"
	switch tier {
	case "Gold":
		fmt.Println("Unlimited books. No late fees.")
	case "Silver":
		fmt.Println("10 books at a time.")
	case "Bronze":
		fmt.Println("5 books at a time.")
	default:
		fmt.Println("Basic membership — 2 books at a time.")
	}
}
successMessage: switch is if-else but readable. The default case catches anything that didn't match — like else at the end.

title: Multiple values in one case
instruction:
Sometimes several different genres should get the same treatment.

Instead of writing the same code in three separate cases, you can combine them:

`case "Sci-Fi", "Fantasy":`

Add a new case to the switch that matches both `"Overdue"` and `"Lost"` and prints `"Fine applies — visit the front desk"`
hint: case "Overdue", "Lost":  — comma-separated values in one case line. Add it before the default case.
expectedOutput: Fine applies — visit the front desk

codeChecks.message: Add a case that matches both "Overdue" and "Lost" using a comma: case "Overdue", "Lost":
Print something about a fine or fee in your new case.
Replace the TODO comment with your case.

successMessage: One case, multiple values. No repetition. Go is sensible like that.

title: switch with no condition
instruction:
A `switch` does not need a variable to compare. Without one, each case acts like an `if` condition.

This is cleaner than a long `else if` chain when each condition is different.

```
switch {
case daysOverdue >= 30:
    fmt.Println("Suspended borrowing privileges")
case daysOverdue >= 14:
    fmt.Println("Overdue notice sent")
}
```

Run this. Change `daysOverdue` to 7 and run again.
hint: Change daysOverdue to 7 and see which case matches.
starter:
package main

import "fmt"

func main() {
	daysOverdue := 45
	switch {
	case daysOverdue >= 30:
		fmt.Println("Suspended borrowing privileges")
	case daysOverdue >= 14:
		fmt.Println("Overdue notice sent")
	case daysOverdue >= 1:
		fmt.Println("Gentle reminder — please return soon")
	default:
		fmt.Println("No overdue items. Thank you!")
	}
}
successMessage: switch without a variable is just a cleaner if-else. Same logic, easier to read.

title: Short variable declaration in if
instruction:
Go lets you declare a variable right inside the `if` statement.

```
if maxBooks := 10 - booksOut; maxBooks > 0 {
    fmt.Println("You can borrow more books")
}
```

The variable is declared, then the condition checks it. It only exists inside the `if` block — nowhere else.

Add an `else` branch that prints `"Borrowing limit reached"` when the limit is not positive.
hint: } else { fmt.Println("Borrowing limit reached") }  — add this right after the closing brace of the if block.

codeChecks.message: Add an else block after the if block.
Print "Borrowing limit reached" inside the else block.
Replace the TODO comment with an else block.

successMessage: Declare, check, use — all in one line. The variable stays scoped to the if block and does not pollute the rest of your function.

title: Nested if — a decision inside a decision
instruction:
Sometimes you need to check one thing, and only if that passes, check something else.

You can put an `if` inside another `if`. Just be careful — too many levels and the code becomes hard to read.

Run this. Try different combinations of `isRegistered` and `hasOutstandingFines`.
hint: Try isRegistered = false, then isRegistered = true and hasOutstandingFines = true, then both good.
expectedOutput: No fines on your account — happy reading!
successMessage: Nested ifs work fine for 2 levels. Past that, consider combining conditions with && to keep it flat.

title: Fix the broken logic
instruction:
This program is supposed to check if someone can borrow a rare book from the library's special collection.

Rules: must be at least 18 AND have a valid membership card.

But the logic is wrong and the program gives incorrect results. Find the bug and fix it.
hint: Check the operator. Should it be && or ||? And check the age threshold.

codeChecks.message: Both conditions must be true. Use && not ||.

successMessage: || means either condition makes it true. && means both must be true. Mixing them up is one of the most common logic bugs — now you know to look for it.

title: Build a decision machine
instruction:
No starter code.

Write a program that has three variables:
- `age` (int) — set it to any value
- `itemsBorrowed` (int) — set it to any value
- `hasMembership` (bool)

Then print:
- `"Premium"` if itemsBorrowed >= 20 AND hasMembership is true
- `"Senior"` if age >= 65
- `"Regular"` if itemsBorrowed >= 5
- `"Newcomer"` otherwise

Use if, else if, and at least one &&.
hint: Start with the strictest condition first (Premium requires both items AND membership). Then work down.

codeChecks.message: Use at least one else if.
Print at least one of: Premium, Senior, Regular, or Newcomer.

successMessage: You built a program that thinks. Every app you have ever used is full of logic exactly like this.
